review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for reinforcement questions on the H100 memory hierarchy, tensor-core throughput, and mixed-precision pitfalls. The key evidence is verbal and does not require a clip.

question_bank.question: Which memory order goes from closest and fastest to farthest and largest in an H100-style hierarchy?
question_bank.explanation: The hierarchy places the smallest and fastest storage nearest the compute units, then larger but slower levels farther away.

transcript_quotes: First, we see we have 256 kilobytes of L1 cache
So this continues the trend of the memory hierarchy in the GPU.

question_bank.question: Why do tensor cores dominate throughput in modern training GPUs?
choices: They execute fixed matrix-multiply tiles at much higher FLOPs per cycle than ordinary FP32 cores
They replace the need for caches
They only help during inference, not training
question_bank.explanation: Deep learning workloads are dominated by matrix multiplications, and tensor cores are specialized to execute those operations far more efficiently than standard FP32 cores.

transcript_quotes: of memory hierarchy in the H100.

question_bank.question: Why can forgetting to cast a PyTorch model to 16-bit make training dramatically slower on an H100?
choices: It forces the workload onto slower FP32 execution paths instead of tensor-core mixed-precision paths
It prevents any memory from reaching HBM
question_bank.explanation: Mixed-precision tensor-core paths are the high-throughput route for modern training. Leaving the model in FP32 can route work to slower hardware paths and severely reduce speed.

transcript_quotes: if you forget to cast your model into 16-bit,
and produce the outputs in a higher precision 32-bit.
